expanded_description: This comprehensive review consolidates the full Boundaries and Grammar domain, integrating the skills of establishing correct sentence boundaries, using punctuation accurately, maintaining subject-verb agreement and verb tense consistency, employing correct pronoun forms, and constructing sentences with properly placed modifiers and parallel structure. These skills represent the core conventions of standard written English that the SAT tests in the Writing and Language section. They are not isolated rules to be memorized but interconnected principles that govern how sentences are built, punctuated, and made clear. This review provides a final diagnostic practice opportunity with passages and questions spanning the full range of grammatical concepts, requiring students to diagnose errors, evaluate corrections, and select the grammatically sound version of each sentence.

core_concept.principle:
The Boundaries and Grammar domain rests on a single foundational insight: the sentence is the fundamental unit of written communication, and every sentence must be constructed according to shared conventions that ensure clarity, precision, and mutual understanding between writer and reader. These conventions are not arbitrary rules; they are the evolved solutions to the recurring communicative challenges that all writers face—how to mark where one thought ends and another begins, how to show relationships among ideas, how to ensure that subjects and verbs are properly linked, how to make clear what pronouns refer to, and how to arrange descriptive material so that it attaches to the right words.

The domain integrates four clusters of skills into a unified grammatical competence:

(1) The Sentence Boundary Cluster: Recognizing and correcting sentence fragments, run-on sentences, and comma splices, and using the appropriate punctuation (periods, semicolons, commas, colons) and conjunctions to establish legal boundaries between clauses. This cluster ensures that every sentence is a grammatically complete unit and that the boundaries between units are properly marked.

(2) The Internal Punctuation Cluster: Using semicolons and colons correctly within sentences, deploying subordinating conjunctions to create complex sentences with correct punctuation, and managing the punctuation that signals the logical architecture of prose. This cluster governs the internal structure of sentences, ensuring that the relationships among clauses are clear and grammatically sound.

(3) The Agreement and Consistency Cluster: Maintaining subject-verb agreement across single and compound subjects, using the correct verb tenses and tense sequences to express temporal relationships, and ensuring pronoun-antecedent agreement and correct pronoun case. This cluster governs the grammatical core of the sentence—the subject and verb—and the referential links that tie sentences together.

(4) The Modifier and Structure Cluster: Placing modifiers correctly to avoid ambiguity and illogic, and constructing parallel structures that express coordinate ideas in coordinate forms. This cluster governs the descriptive and organizational dimensions of sentences, ensuring that sentences are not only grammatically correct but clear and stylistically effective.
core_concept.explanation: These four clusters are not independent domains of knowledge; they are different facets of the single act of constructing a sentence. A well-formed sentence has legal boundaries (Cluster 1), clear internal punctuation (Cluster 2), properly agreeing and consistent grammatical elements (Cluster 3), and logically placed and parallel modifiers (Cluster 4). Every SAT Writing and Language question tests one or more of these facets, and the skilled test-taker learns to see each sentence as a whole, diagnosing whatever problem it may contain regardless of which cluster the error falls into. The review that follows provides integrated practice in which students must apply the full range of their grammatical knowledge to identify and correct errors of many types, often within a single passage.

image.alt: A central hub labeled 'Grammatical Competence' with four interconnected rings: Sentence Boundaries (fragments, run-ons, comma splices), Internal Punctuation (semicolons, colons, subordinating conjunctions), Agreement and Consistency (subject-verb, verb tense, pronouns), and Modifiers and Structure (misplaced modifiers, dangling modifiers, parallelism). Arrows show interconnections among all rings.
image.caption: The Boundaries and Grammar domain: four skill clusters integrated into a unified competence in constructing clear, correct sentences

sections.section_title: Review of Core Rules and Error Types
sections.introduction: This section consolidates the most important rules, error types, and correction strategies from each lesson in the Boundaries and Grammar domain. Students should review this summary to reinforce their command of standard written English conventions and identify any areas where additional practice is needed.

key_concept.definition:
The Boundaries and Grammar domain comprises ten lessons, each addressing a specific set of conventions within the four clusters. Mastery of the domain requires knowing both the rules and the characteristic error patterns that the SAT uses to test them.

6.1 Sentence Boundaries Introduction: Complete sentences require an independent clause. Fragments lack an independent clause; run-ons join independent clauses without legal punctuation. The three major boundary errors are fragments, fused sentences, and comma splices.

6.2 Run-On Sentences: Run-ons occur when independent clauses are fused with no punctuation or joined with only a comma. The four legal corrections are: period, semicolon, comma + FANBOYS, or subordination.

6.3 Comma Splices: A comma splice joins independent clauses with only a comma. The same four corrections apply, with special attention to the distinction between a comma splice and correct comma uses (lists, introductory phrases, nonrestrictive clauses).

6.4 Semicolons and Colons: Semicolons join independent clauses or separate complex list items. Colons introduce explanations, lists, or quotations after an independent clause. The material before a colon must be an independent clause.

6.5 Subordinating Conjunctions: Words like because, although, while, if, and when create dependent clauses and signal logical relationships. Introductory dependent clauses require a following comma.

6.6 Subject-Verb Agreement: The verb must agree with the true subject in number. Prepositional phrase interference is the most common trap: nouns inside prepositional phrases are never the subject.

6.7 Verb Tense and Form: Tenses must accurately express temporal relationships. The past perfect marks the earlier of two past actions. Irregular verb forms must be correct, especially the past participle after auxiliaries.

6.8 Pronoun Usage: Pronouns must agree with their antecedents in number and gender, be in the correct case (subjective, objective, possessive), and refer clearly and unambiguously to their antecedents.

6.9 Modifiers and Parallelism: Modifiers must be placed next to the words they modify and have a logical subject. Coordinate elements must be in the same grammatical form.

6.10 Review: Integrating all grammar skills to diagnose and correct errors across all categories.
key_concept.context: This review serves as a final reference before the comprehensive practice. Students should use it to ensure they can recognize each error type and recall the appropriate correction strategy.

categories.description: A consolidated reference of all major error types from the Boundaries and Grammar domain.

examples.explanation:
Fragment: Incomplete sentence. Missing independent clause. Often a dependent clause or a phrase standing alone. Correction: Attach to an independent clause or supply the missing element.

Fused Sentence: Two independent clauses with no punctuation between them. Correction: Insert period, semicolon, or comma + FANBOYS.

Comma Splice: Two independent clauses joined only by a comma. Correction: Replace comma with period or semicolon, add FANBOYS, or subordinate one clause.

Semicolon Error: Using a semicolon where one side is not an independent clause, or using a semicolon where a colon is needed for explanation.

Colon Error: Using a colon where the preceding material is not an independent clause, or using a colon to separate a verb from its object or a preposition from its object.

Subordination Punctuation Error: Missing comma after an introductory dependent clause, or unnecessary comma before a dependent clause that follows the main clause.

Subject-Verb Agreement: Verb does not match the true subject in number. Often caused by prepositional phrase interference or misidentification of the subject.

Verb Tense: Wrong tense for the temporal context, inconsistent tense shift, missing past perfect for earlier of two past actions, incorrect irregular verb form after auxiliary.

Pronoun Agreement: Pronoun does not match antecedent in number or gender. Often with indefinite pronouns (each, every) or compound antecedents.

Pronoun Case: Subjective form used for object, or objective form used for subject. Often in compound constructions (between you and I).

Pronoun Clarity: Ambiguous reference (two possible antecedents) or missing antecedent.

Misplaced Modifier: Modifier placed next to the wrong word, creating ambiguity. Correction: Move the modifier next to the intended word.

Dangling Modifier: Introductory modifier has no logical subject in the main clause. Correction: Change the main clause subject or restructure the modifier.

Faulty Parallelism: Coordinate elements are not in the same grammatical form. Correction: Make all elements match.

image.alt: A comprehensive table with columns for Error Category, Specific Error, Example, and Correction, covering all error types in the domain.
image.caption: The complete error catalog for the Boundaries and Grammar domain: a reference for diagnostic analysis and correction

sections.introduction: This section provides an extended SAT-style passage with underlined portions that contain a variety of grammatical errors spanning the full Boundaries and Grammar domain. Students must diagnose each error and select the correct answer from among the options provided.

key_concept.definition: The comprehensive practice passage integrates errors from all four grammar clusters, requiring students to shift among different types of grammatical analysis within a single passage. The strategic approach: (1) Read each underlined portion carefully. (2) Diagnose the error (or confirm that there is no error). (3) Recall the correction strategy appropriate to that error type. (4) Evaluate the answer choices and select the one that corrects the error without introducing a new problem. (5) Verify that the chosen correction is consistent with the surrounding context and maintains the intended meaning.
key_concept.context: This passage-based practice simulates the integrated nature of the SAT Writing and Language section, where grammar questions are embedded within longer texts that students must read for coherence as well as correctness.

categories.type: Practice Passage with Integrated Grammar Errors
categories.description: A passage with multiple underlined portions, each testing a different grammatical concept.

examples.explanation:
Passage: 'The discovery of gravitational waves in 2015 confirmed a major prediction of Einstein's general theory of relativity. [1] These ripples in the fabric of spacetime, caused by some of the most violent events in the universe such as the collision of black holes. [2] The detection was made by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO), a facility that uses lasers to measure incredibly small disturbances, their precision is measured in fractions of a proton's diameter. [3] Because the signals are so faint that distinguishing them from background noise requires sophisticated algorithms. [4] The team of physicists and engineers who operate LIGO were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2017. [5] Neither the lead researchers nor the director of the observatory were expecting the discovery to come so soon after the facility became operational. [6] Each of the scientists involved in the project have contributed to an achievement that opens an entirely new window on the cosmos.'

Questions 1-6 target the underlined portions numbered [1] through [6].

Underlined [1]: 'These ripples in the fabric of spacetime, caused by some of the most violent events in the universe such as the collision of black holes.' This is a fragment. The subject 'ripples' is followed by modifiers but has no main verb. Correction: Attach to the previous sentence or supply a verb. A possible correction: 'These ripples in the fabric of spacetime are caused by some of the most violent events in the universe, such as the collision of black holes.' Or attach: '...relativity, these ripples in the fabric of spacetime being caused by...' The SAT answer choices would offer specific corrections. The error type is a fragment (missing main verb).

Underlined [2]: '...small disturbances, their precision is measured in fractions of a proton's diameter.' Two independent clauses joined by a comma: 'a facility that uses lasers to measure incredibly small disturbances' and 'their precision is measured in fractions of a proton's diameter.' This is a comma splice. Correction options: semicolon, period, comma + 'and,' or restructuring. Possible correction: '...small disturbances; their precision is measured in fractions of a proton's diameter.'

Underlined [3]: 'Because the signals are so faint that distinguishing them from background noise requires sophisticated algorithms.' This is a dependent clause fragment—'Because' makes it dependent, and there is no main clause. Correction: Attach to the previous or following sentence, or remove 'Because.' Possible correction: 'The signals are so faint that distinguishing them from background noise requires sophisticated algorithms.' (Remove subordinator.)

Underlined [4]: 'The team of physicists and engineers who operate LIGO were awarded the Nobel Prize...' The subject is 'team,' which is a collective noun. The prepositional phrase 'of physicists and engineers' modifies 'team.' The true subject is singular ('team'), so the verb should be 'was awarded,' not 'were awarded.' (Unless the passage treats 'team' as plural, which is more common in British English but not standard American English. The SAT follows American convention: collective nouns are usually singular.) Correction: 'The team... was awarded.'

examples.process: Question 5: Subject-Verb Agreement with Compound Subject
examples.explanation: Underlined [5]: 'Neither the lead researchers nor the director of the observatory were expecting the discovery...' The compound subject is 'Neither the lead researchers nor the director.' With 'neither...nor,' the verb agrees with the nearer subject. The nearer subject is 'director,' which is singular. The verb should be 'was expecting,' not 'were expecting.' Correction: '...nor the director of the observatory was expecting...'

examples.process: Question 6: Subject-Verb Agreement with Indefinite Pronoun
examples.explanation: Underlined [6]: 'Each of the scientists involved in the project have contributed to an achievement...' The subject is 'Each,' a singular indefinite pronoun. The prepositional phrase 'of the scientists' does not affect agreement. The verb should be 'has contributed,' not 'have contributed.' Correction: 'Each of the scientists... has contributed...'

image.alt: An annotated practice passage with each underlined portion labeled by error type and the correct answer choice explained.
image.caption: Comprehensive grammar practice: a passage integrating multiple error types from all four clusters of the Boundaries and Grammar domain

summary:
The Boundaries and Grammar domain consolidates the conventions of standard written English that govern sentence construction, punctuation, agreement, and clarity. The domain's ten lessons build from the foundational unit of the independent clause through the punctuation and conjunctions that connect clauses, to the internal grammatical relationships that ensure correctness and coherence. Together, they constitute the grammatical competence that the SAT measures and that effective writing demands.

The unified approach to any grammar question follows a diagnostic sequence: identify the error type by examining the grammatical structure of the sentence, recall the rule that has been violated, and apply the appropriate correction. For boundary errors, the diagnostic question is whether each group of words punctuated as a sentence actually contains a legal independent clause, and whether the punctuation between clauses is legally sufficient. For internal punctuation, the question is whether semicolons and colons are used according to their specific rules, and whether dependent and independent clauses are correctly punctuated relative to each other. For agreement and consistency, the question is whether subjects and verbs agree, tenses are logically sequenced, and pronouns match their antecedents in number, gender, and case. For modifiers and parallelism, the question is whether descriptive elements are correctly placed and logically connected, and whether coordinate structures are grammatically symmetrical.

The integrated practice passage demonstrates how these error types co-occur in real passages and require flexible diagnostic skills. A single passage may contain a fragment, a comma splice, a subject-verb agreement error, and a pronoun case error in close proximity. The skilled reader moves fluidly among the different diagnostic frameworks, applying the appropriate analysis to each underlined portion. This flexibility, grounded in a comprehensive command of the conventions of standard written English, is the ultimate goal of the Boundaries and Grammar domain and the foundation of competent, confident writing in every context.
